questions.question: What are the five sections of the Complete Sales Playbook?

options.text: One-Page System, Situation Playbook, 90-Day Contract, Quarterly Review, Decision Log
Prospecting, Qualifying, Presenting, Closing, Following Up

questions.explanation: The five sections are: One-Page Acquisition System (Lesson 7), Situation-Specific Playbook (Lessons 2-6), 90-Day Commitment Contract (Lesson 8), Quarterly Review Framework (Lesson 9), and Decision Log (tracking what changed and why).

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 14-Day Execution Sprint?

options.text: To get the assembled playbook into motion with immediate execution
To hire your first sales rep

questions.explanation: The 14-Day Execution Sprint bridges the gap between having a completed playbook and actually executing it. It's the ignition that gets the system in motion.

questions.question: What separates founders who completed this course from 95% of other founders?

options.text: They built a specific, named, signed, wall-mounted commitment that turns insight into action
They spend more time on social media

questions.explanation: Most founders read articles, watch videos, and collect frameworks but never build the specific, committed system. Building and signing a commitment that you execute daily separates knowledge consumers from action takers.

questions.question: Once your Complete Sales Playbook is assembled, it never needs updating.
questions.explanation: False. The playbook is a living document. It gets versioned like software — v1.0 at launch, refined at 30 days, strategically updated at 90 days. The quarterly review framework ensures it stays current with real-world data.

questions.question: Which of the five Complete Playbook sections do you have ready? Which are incomplete? What is your plan for the 14-Day Execution Sprint to launch your system?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response honestly assesses each of the five sections, identifies gaps, and commits to a specific 14-day sprint plan with concrete daily activities and a launch date — demonstrating readiness to move from planning to execution.
